perturbation.original_text: 4. All Proprietary Information, including all applicable intellectual property rights residing in the Proprietary Information (including without limitation, patents, copyrights, industrial designs, trademarks and trade secrets), unless otherwise specified in writing by Disclosing Party, are and shall remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, including any and all reproductions in any form of said Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. All Proprietary Information, including all applicable intellectual property rights residing in the Proprietary Information (including without limitation, patents, copyrights, industrial designs, trademarks and trade secrets), unless otherwise specified in writing by Disclosing Party, are and shall remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, including any and all reproductions in any form of said Proprietary Information. However, the Recipient shall have a non-exclusive license to use the Proprietary Information for internal research purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by stating that all Proprietary Information remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party, while also granting the Recipient a non-exclusive license to use it for internal research. This contradicts the initial claim of exclusive ownership and creates ambiguity about the extent of the Disclosing Party's rights. It contradicts the ownership clause of the agreement as laid out in section 4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. All Proprietary Information, including all applicable intellectual property rights residing in the Proprietary Information (including without limitation, patents, copyrights, industrial designs, trademarks and trade secrets), unless otherwise specified in writing by Disclosing Party, are and shall remain the exclusive property of Disclosing Party, including any and all reproductions in any form of said Proprietary Information. However, the Recipient shall have a non-exclusive license to use the Proprietary Information for internal research purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text states that the Disclosing Party retains exclusive property rights over the Proprietary Information. However, the changed text then grants the Recipient a non-exclusive license to use the same Proprietary Information for internal research purposes. This creates a direct conflict because 'exclusive property' implies that only the Disclosing Party has the right to use the information, while the license grants the Recipient usage rights, thereby diminishing the Disclosing Party's exclusivity. This contradiction is also reflected in the contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Recipient hereby agrees that it will not use the Proprietary Information for any reason or purpose other than to evaluate a possible Business Arrangement with the Disclosing Party and that the use of the Proprietary Information supplied hereunder shall be limited to Recipient's activities in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Recipient hereby agrees that it will not use the Proprietary Information for any reason or purpose other than to evaluate a possible Business Arrangement with the Disclosing Party and that the use of the Proprietary Information supplied hereunder shall be limited to Recipient's activities in connection therewith. However, the Recipient is allowed to use the Proprietary Information to check if the Disclosing Party infringes any of its patents.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by stating that Proprietary Information must be used to evaluate a possible Business Arrangement, and then permits the Recipient to use the information to check for patent infringements. It contradicts the defined purpose for using Proprietary Information, creating uncertainty about whether checking for patent infringements constitutes a breach of contract. This in-text contradiction changes the agreed-upon uses of the Proprietary Information as laid out in section 6 of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Recipient hereby agrees that it will not use the Proprietary Information for any reason or purpose other than to evaluate a possible Business Arrangement with the Disclosing Party and that the use of the Proprietary Information supplied hereunder shall be limited to Recipient's activities in connection therewith. However, the Recipient is allowed to use the Proprietary Information to check if the Disclosing Party infringes any of its patents.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows the Recipient to use Proprietary Information to check for patent infringements, which directly contradicts the original purpose stated in the first part of section 6, which is to 'evaluate a possible Business Arrangement.' This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the changed text broadens the acceptable use of the Proprietary Information beyond the initial constraint. The contradicted text is also section 6.

perturbation.original_text: 8. In the event that Recipient violates this Agreement or Disclosing Party has reasonable cause to feel that its Proprietary Information is in danger of being disclosed or misused, either Party may at any time, in addition to all other recourse under law, terminate this Agreement and demand and be entitled to the return from Recipient of all Proprietary Information in all existing forms.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. In the event that Recipient violates this Agreement or Disclosing Party has reasonable cause to feel that its Proprietary Information is in danger of being disclosed or misused, either Party may at any time, in addition to all other recourse under law, terminate this Agreement and demand and be entitled to the return from Recipient of all Proprietary Information in all existing forms. However, the Disclosing party must grant the Recipient a 30-day grace period to remedy the breach.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by stating that either party may terminate the agreement if it is violated. However, it then requires the Disclosing Party to grant a 30-day grace period, creating uncertainty regarding the conditions of termination. It contradicts the immediate termination provision, raising questions about when termination can occur. This pertubation adds a conflicting process regarding termination as outlined in section 8.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. In the event that Recipient violates this Agreement or Disclosing Party has reasonable cause to feel that its Proprietary Information is in danger of being disclosed or misused, either Party may at any time, in addition to all other recourse under law, terminate this Agreement and demand and be entitled to the return from Recipient of all Proprietary Information in all existing forms. However, the Disclosing party must grant the Recipient a 30-day grace period to remedy the breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows either party to terminate the agreement immediately upon a violation or reasonable cause. The changed text adds a 30-day grace period for the Recipient to remedy the breach. This directly contradicts the original provision for immediate termination, creating an inconsistency as outlined in the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition. The Contradicted Text and Changed Text exist in the same location, but the change introduces a conflict as the Disclosing party cannot immediately terminate the agreement as the original text states.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Each Party agrees that until the expiry of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, each Party or its parent and affiliated companies (herein, collectively a "Party"), will not, and each Party will direct its parent and affiliated companies not to, except with the prior written consent of the other Party (i) purchase, acquire, obtain or hold (or offer or agree to purchase, acquire, obtain or hold) beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any of the other Party's securities; (iii) enter into, or agree to enter into or seek or propose any merger, consolidation, business combination, tender or exchange offer, sale or purchase of assets or securities, dissolution, liquidation, restructuring, recapitalization or similar transaction involving the other Party; (iv) act, alone or together with any person, to control or influence the management or the Board of Directors or other Party; or (v) advise, encourage, or assist any person in the acquisition of beneficial ownership of any of the other Party's securities or control of the other Party or a significant portion of its significant assets; provided, however, that such restrictions (or the restrictions set forth in the next sentence) shall terminate in the event that a third party publicly commences a tender or exchange offer, or otherwise proposes a merger or business combination transaction, in respect of the other Party and such other Party has (a) exempted such transaction for purposes of Section 203 of the Delaware General Corporation Law, or (b) publicly announced or publicly confirmed that it is engaged in negotiations in connection with such proposed transaction; provided, further, any such termination shall not relieve the parties of any obligations hereunder with respect to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Each Party agrees that until the expiry of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, each Party or its parent and affiliated companies (herein, collectively a "Party"), will not, and each Party will direct its parent and affiliated companies not to, except with the prior written consent of the other Party (i) purchase, acquire, obtain or hold (or offer or agree to purchase, acquire, obtain or hold) beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any of the other Party's securities; (iii) enter into, or agree to enter into or seek or propose any merger, consolidation, business combination, tender or exchange offer, sale or purchase of assets or securities, dissolution, liquidation, restructuring, recapitalization or similar transaction involving the other Party; (iv) act, alone or together with any person, to control or influence the management or the Board of Directors or other Party; or (v) advise, encourage, or assist any person in the acquisition of beneficial ownership of any of the other Party's securities or control of the other Party or a significant portion of its significant assets; provided, however, that such restrictions (or the restrictions set forth in the next sentence) shall terminate in the event that a third party publicly commences a tender or exchange offer, or otherwise proposes a merger or business combination transaction, in respect of the other Party and such other Party has (a) exempted such transaction for purposes of Section 203 of the Delaware General Corporation Law, or (b) publicly announced or publicly confirmed that it is engaged in negotiations in connection with such proposed transaction; provided, further, any such termination shall not relieve the parties of any obligations hereunder with respect to the Proprietary Information. However, this section does not apply to investments of less than $100,000.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by imposing restrictions on the acquisition of securities. However, a contradictory exception is introduced: 'However, this section does not apply to investments of less than $100,000.' This creates ambiguity by allowing small investments which are otherwise restricted, contradicting the general prohibition. The inserted text introduces a conflicting condition that compromises the overall obligations presented in section 11.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Each Party agrees that until the expiry of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, each Party or its parent and affiliated companies (herein, collectively a "Party"), will not, and each Party will direct its parent and affiliated companies not to, except with the prior written consent of the other Party (i) purchase, acquire, obtain or hold (or offer or agree to purchase, acquire, obtain or hold) beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any of the other Party's securities; (iii) enter into, or agree to enter into or seek or propose any merger, consolidation, business combination, tender or exchange offer, sale or purchase of assets or securities, dissolution, liquidation, restructuring, recapitalization or similar transaction involving the other Party; (iv) act, alone or together with any person, to control or influence the management or the Board of Directors or other Party; or (v) advise, encourage, or assist any person in the acquisition of beneficial ownership of any of the other Party's securities or control of the other Party or a significant portion of its significant assets; provided, however, that such restrictions (or the restrictions set forth in the next sentence) shall terminate in the event that a third party publicly commences a tender or exchange offer, or otherwise proposes a merger or business combination transaction, in respect of the other Party and such other Party has (a) exempted such transaction for purposes of Section 203 of the Delaware General Corporation Law, or (b) publicly announced or publicly confirmed that it is engaged in negotiations in connection with such proposed transaction; provided, further, any such termination shall not relieve the parties of any obligations hereunder with respect to the Proprietary Information. However, this section does not apply to investments of less than $100,000.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the restrictions outlined in Section 11, stating that the section does not apply to investments of less than $100,000. This directly contradicts the initial agreement that 'each Party will not... purchase, acquire, obtain or hold beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party'. The exception creates a clear inconsistency and conflict within the document, making the enforcement of the original restriction ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: 15. The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive for a period of three (3) years after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive for a period of three (3) years after the termination of this Agreement, unless the Disclosing Party consents in writing to an earlier termination of these provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text says that the terms pertaining to Proprietary Information survive for three years after termination. The added text introduces a way to terminate this survival period earlier, which creates an in-text contradiction. There is no longer a consistent survival period, which contradicts the original terms of section 15.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15. The provisions of this Agreement pertaining to Proprietary Information disclosed under this Agreement shall continue and survive for a period of three (3) years after the termination of this Agreement, unless the Disclosing Party consents in writing to an earlier termination of these provisions.
perturbation.justification: The original text states a fixed survival period of three years for provisions related to Proprietary Information. The changed text introduces an exception allowing for earlier termination with the Disclosing Party's consent. This creates a contradiction because the survival period is no longer consistently three years, thus contradicting the original obligation as outlined in the Perturbation Explanation and falling under the definition of inconsistencies causing in-text contradictions.
